## Short conclusion

I can rigorously identify the Bounebache–Zambotti (BZ) Dirichlet-form process with the ABLM \(\delta_0\)-drift solution **under the extra Dirichlet-extension assumptions you stated**, for initial law \(\nu\), and for all deterministic initial points outside the BZ exceptional set. This gives
\[
R_\lambda^P(x,\cdot)\ll \nu
\quad\text{for quasi-every }x.
\]
I do **not** see a proof, from the assumptions as stated, that upgrades this to **every** deterministic \(x\in E\). The precise bottleneck is: one must prove that the ABLM solution started from an arbitrary deterministic \(x\) instantly avoids the BZ exceptional set, or prove an equivalent strong-Feller / uniform-density statement.

---

# 1. Matching BZ with the \(\delta_0\)-drift SPDE

Let
\[
f(r)=-2\,{\bf 1}_{\{r>0\}}.
\]
This is bounded and of bounded variation. In BZ notation,
\[
F(x)=\int_0^1 f(x_\theta)\,d\theta
   =-2\int_0^1{\bf 1}_{\{x_\theta>0\}}\,d\theta,
\]
so their Gibbs measure is
\[
\nu(dx)=Z^{-1}e^{-F(x)}\gamma(dx)
=Z^{-1}\exp\!\left(2\int_0^1{\bf 1}_{\{x_\theta>0\}}\,d\theta\right)\gamma(dx),
\]
which is exactly the expected invariant law. BZ define this class of Gibbs measures for bounded BV \(f\), using \(\gamma\) as the Brownian-bridge law, and associate it with the gradient Dirichlet form
\[
\mathcal E(\Phi,\Psi)=\frac12\int_H\langle\nabla\Phi,\nabla\Psi\rangle_H\,d\nu.
\]
Their paper explicitly treats Dirichlet boundary conditions \(u(t,0)=u(t,1)=0\) and local-time drifts of the form \(-\frac12\int f(da)\,\partial_\theta \ell^a_{t,\theta}\). ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/1105.2779))

For this \(f\), in the distributional sense,
\[
f'=-2\delta_0,
\qquad
-\frac12 f'=\delta_0.
\]
Equivalently, in the BZ local-time weak formulation, for \(h\in C_c^2(0,1)\),
\[
\frac12\int_{\mathbb R} f(da)\int_0^1 h'(\theta)\ell^a_{s,\theta}\,d\theta
=
-\int_0^1 h'(\theta)\ell^0_{s,\theta}\,d\theta.
\]
By the spatial occupation/local-time identity,
\[
-\int_0^1 h'(\theta)\ell^0_{s,\theta}\,d\theta
=
\int_0^1 h(\theta)\,\delta_0(u_s(\theta))\,d\theta
\]
in the distributional sense. Thus BZ’s weak formulation becomes
\[
\langle u_t-u_\varepsilon,h\rangle
=
\frac12\int_\varepsilon^t\langle u_s,h''\rangle\,ds
+
\int_\varepsilon^t\langle \delta_0(u_s),h\rangle\,ds
+
\int_\varepsilon^t\int_0^1 h(\theta)\,W(ds,d\theta).
\]
This is the analytically weak form of
\[
\partial_t u=\frac12\partial_{\theta\theta}u+\delta_0(u)+\dot W.
\]
BZ prove the associated quasi-regular Dirichlet-form process is a weak solution of their local-time equation. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/1105.2779))

Now compare with ABLM. Their distributional-drift solution is defined by approximation of the drift \(b\) by smooth \(b_n\), with convergence of the drift term in the mild formulation. For smooth \(b\), their definition reduces to the usual mild formulation. They prove strong uniqueness for \(b=\kappa\delta_0\), \(\kappa\in\mathbb R\), and stability of smooth approximations. However, the published ABLM paper explicitly does **not** treat Dirichlet boundary conditions; it treats \(\mathbb R\), periodic, and Neumann cases, and says the Dirichlet case should require extra work because a key local-nondeterminism bound degenerates near the boundary. So the identification below uses exactly your “Dirichlet-extension assumptions.” ([eprints.whiterose.ac.uk](https://eprints.whiterose.ac.uk/206080/7/Comm%20Pure%20Appl%20Math%20-%202023%20-%20Athreya%20-%20Well%E2%80%90posedness%20of%20stochastic%20heat%20equation%20with%20distributional%20drift%20and%20skew.pdf))

**Identification lemma.** Assume the ABLM strong well-posedness, stability, and weak/mild equivalence extend to the Dirichlet state space \(E=C_0([0,1])\). Then:

1. The BZ stationary process with initial law \(\nu\) is an ABLM weak solution with \(b=\delta_0\).
2. By ABLM uniqueness in law, its law equals the ABLM solution law started from \(\nu\).
3. Therefore \(\nu\) is invariant for the ABLM semigroup \(P_t\).
4. There is a BZ exceptional set \(N\) of zero \(\mathcal E\)-capacity such that for every \(x\in E\setminus N\), the BZ process started from \(x\) is identified with the ABLM solution started from \(x\). Hence
\[
R_\lambda^P(x,\cdot)\ll \nu,
\qquad x\in E\setminus N.
\]

The last point uses BZ’s resolvent absolute-continuity theorem. Importantly, their proof constructs the actual Markov kernel outside an exceptional set; on the exceptional set they set the resolvent kernel to zero for the operator-level statement. Thus BZ gives quasi-everywhere absolute continuity, not automatically actual absolute continuity for every deterministic initial condition. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/1105.2779))

---

# 2. Attempts to upgrade to every deterministic \(x\)

## 2(a). BZ resolvent AC plus ABLM uniqueness

What works:

\[
R_\lambda^P(x,\cdot)\ll\nu
\quad\text{for all }x\in E\setminus N.
\]

What is missing:

\[
P_t(x,N)=0
\quad\text{for all }x\in E,\ t>0.
\]

Indeed, this “instantaneous escape from the BZ exceptional set” would be enough.

**Lemma.** Suppose \(N\) is the BZ exceptional set and:

- for \(y\notin N\), \(R_\lambda^P(y,\cdot)\ll\nu\);
- for every \(x\in E\) and \(s>0\), \(P_s(x,N)=0\).

Then \(R_\lambda^P(x,\cdot)\ll\nu\) for every \(x\in E\).

**Proof.** Let \(B\) be Borel with \(\nu(B)=0\). For \(s>0\),
\[
R_\lambda^P{\bf 1}_B(x)
=
\int_0^s e^{-\lambda t}P_t{\bf 1}_B(x)\,dt
+
e^{-\lambda s}\,\mathbf E_x\big[R_\lambda^P{\bf 1}_B(X_s)\big].
\]
The first term is at most \(s\). The second term is zero because \(X_s\notin N\) a.s. and \(R_\lambda^P(y,B)=0\) for \(y\notin N\). Letting \(s\downarrow0\) gives \(R_\lambda^P(x,B)=0\). ∎

Thus the cleanest missing lemma is:

\[
\boxed{\text{For every BZ exceptional }N,\quad P_t(x,N)=0\text{ for all }x,t>0.}
\]

I do not see a proof of this from the current assumptions alone. A strong-Feller or resolvent-strong-Feller theorem would also remove the exceptional set, because \(R_\lambda{\bf 1}_B\) would be a continuous function vanishing \(\nu\)-a.e., and \(\nu\) has full support. But BZ explicitly note that strong Feller is not available in their approach. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/1105.2779))

---

## 2(b). Smooth approximants and uniform density bounds

Let \(b_n=-\frac12 f_n'\) be smooth approximations of \(\delta_0\), and let \(P_t^n\) be the corresponding smooth SPDE semigroup with invariant law
\[
\nu_n(dx)=Z_n^{-1}e^{-F_n(x)}\gamma(dx).
\]
For each fixed \(n\), one expects
\[
P_t^n(x,\cdot)\ll \nu_n\sim\nu
\]
for every \(x\), by regular diffusion theory or Girsanov-type arguments.

ABLM stability would give, for each deterministic \(x\),
\[
P_t^n(x,\cdot)\Rightarrow P_t(x,\cdot).
\]
But weak limits of absolutely continuous measures can be singular unless one has a uniform integrability estimate for the densities.

A sufficient criterion is the following.

**Uniform \(L^p\)-criterion.** Suppose that for some \(p>1\),
\[
R_\lambda^{n}(x,dy)=r_{\lambda}^{n}(x,y)\,\nu(dy),
\qquad
\sup_n\|r_\lambda^n(x,\cdot)\|_{L^p(\nu)}<\infty,
\]
and \(R_\lambda^n(x,\cdot)\Rightarrow R_\lambda^P(x,\cdot)\). Then
\[
R_\lambda^P(x,\cdot)\ll\nu.
\]

**Proof.** The densities \(r_\lambda^n\) are weakly compact in \(L^p(\nu)\). Along a subsequence, \(r_\lambda^n\rightharpoonup r\) in \(L^p\). Testing against bounded continuous functions identifies the weak measure limit as \(r\nu\). Therefore the limiting resolvent is absolutely continuous. ∎

The obstacle is that the natural Girsanov estimates depend on \(\|b_n\|_\infty\) or \(\|f_n'\|_\infty\), which blows up for mollifiers approximating \(\delta_0\). Thus this route reduces the problem to a very concrete estimate:

\[
\boxed{
\text{Find }p>1\text{ and prove }\sup_n\|dR_\lambda^n(x)/d\nu\|_{L^p(\nu)}<\infty.
}
\]

I do not see this estimate from ABLM stability alone.

---

## 2(c). What boundedness of the potential does give

Here there is a useful but insufficient estimate. Since the \(f_n\) can be chosen uniformly bounded,
\[
|F_n(x)|\le M
\]
uniformly in \(n,x\). Hence
\[
c\,\gamma\le \nu_n\le C\,\gamma,
\qquad
c\,\nu\le \nu_n\le C\,\nu
\]
with constants independent of \(n\). Consequently the Dirichlet forms
\[
\mathcal E_n(\Phi,\Phi)=\frac12\int\|\nabla\Phi\|_H^2\,d\nu_n
\]
are uniformly comparable to the Gaussian Ornstein–Uhlenbeck form. By the same minimax argument used by BZ, this gives uniform Hilbert–Schmidt bounds of the form
\[
\iint p_t^n(x,y)^2\,\nu_n(dx)\nu_n(dy)
\le C_t<\infty.
\]
BZ use precisely this kind of spectral comparison to prove Hilbert–Schmidt kernel bounds and Mosco convergence for the regularized stationary processes. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/1105.2779))

This is enough to prove the following preservation statement:

**AC preservation for already-AC initial laws.** If \(\eta\ll\nu\), then, under ABLM approximation stability,
\[
\eta R_\lambda^P\ll\nu.
\]

Sketch: first prove it for \(d\eta/d\nu\in L^2(\nu)\) using the \(L^2\)-contraction of the symmetric semigroups \(P_t^n\), uniformly in \(n\), then pass to the limit using the \(L^2\) weak compactness criterion above. General \(L^1\) densities follow by truncation and total-variation approximation.

This is useful, but it does not solve the invariant-measure problem because an arbitrary invariant \(\mu\) is not known to satisfy \(\mu\ll\nu\).

---

## 2(d). Small-time smoothing of the linear heat equation

For the linear Dirichlet stochastic heat equation, the transition law from any deterministic \(x\in E\) is equivalent to the Brownian-bridge law \(\gamma\) at every \(t>0\). This is the usual infinite-dimensional Ornstein–Uhlenbeck Gaussian equivalence: the covariance is equivalent to the invariant covariance and the mean \(S_t x\) lies in the Cameron–Martin space.

A tempting idea is to write the nonlinear solution as
\[
u_t=S_t x+V_t+K_t,
\]
where \(V_t\) is the stochastic convolution and \(K_t\) is the drift contribution. Heat smoothing suggests \(K_t\) may lie in the Cameron–Martin space \(H_0^1\) for \(t>0\). But this is not enough. A random, noise-dependent Cameron–Martin shift does not by itself imply quasi-invariance of the law. One would need an adapted Girsanov shift with finite \(L^2([0,t]\times[0,1])\)-energy, or a Malliavin/nonlinear quasi-invariance theorem. For the \(\delta_0\) drift, the drift is a local-time distribution, not an \(L^2\) space-time function.

So this route again reduces to a genuine missing smoothing theorem:

\[
\boxed{
\text{Show directly that }P_t(x,\cdot)\ll\nu\text{ for }t>0,
\text{ or at least }P_t(x,N)=0.
}
\]

---

# 3. Weaker statements sufficient for invariant measures

The following is enough for uniqueness.

**Invariant-measure lemma.** Let \(N\) be the BZ exceptional set. Assume:

1. \(R_\lambda^P(x,\cdot)\ll\nu\) for every \(x\notin N\);
2. \(\mu(N)=0\) for every invariant probability measure \(\mu\) of \(P_t\).

Then every invariant probability measure \(\mu\) satisfies \(\mu\ll\nu\).

**Proof.** Let \(B\) be Borel with \(\nu(B)=0\). By invariance,
\[
\mu(B)=\lambda\,\mu R_\lambda^P(B)
=\lambda\int_E R_\lambda^P(x,B)\,\mu(dx).
\]
The integrand is zero for \(x\notin N\), and \(\mu(N)=0\). Hence \(\mu(B)=0\). ∎

Once \(\mu\ll\nu\), the standard symmetric-form proof gives \(\mu=\nu\). Indeed, write \(d\mu=h\,d\nu\). Since \(P_t\) agrees \(\nu\)-a.e. with the BZ symmetric semigroup, invariance gives \(P_t h=h\) in \(L^1(\nu)\). The BZ form is the closure of the gradient form and \(\nu\sim\gamma\), so its zero-energy functions are constants; equivalently, the symmetric semigroup is ergodic. Therefore \(h\) is constant and \(\mu=\nu\).

Thus the invariant-measure problem is reduced to:

\[
\boxed{
\text{Prove every invariant }\mu\text{ charges no BZ exceptional set.}
}
\]

A stronger but more checkable condition is the earlier instantaneous-escape property:
\[
P_t(x,N)=0\quad\forall x,t>0.
\]
If this holds, then every invariant \(\mu\) automatically satisfies
\[
\mu(N)=\int P_t(x,N)\,\mu(dx)=0.
\]

I do not see a direct proof of \(\mu(N)=0\) from stationarity alone. Starting with \(X_0\sim\mu\), stationarity only says \(X_t\sim\mu\). Without a smoothing theorem for \(P_t(x,\cdot)\), this gives no information about whether \(\mu\) charges capacity-zero sets.

---

# 4. Precise bottleneck

The current assumptions identify the ABLM semigroup with the BZ symmetric Dirichlet-form semigroup only:

- \(\nu\)-a.e. for stationary initial law;
- quasi-everywhere for deterministic initial points outside a BZ exceptional set.

They do **not** by themselves imply what happens when the ABLM solution starts from a point inside that exceptional set. Since invariant measures may in principle charge sets invisible to \(\nu\), the standard symmetric-form proof cannot be completed without one extra input.

A sufficient extra input is any one of the following:

1. **Full kernel AC**
   \[
   R_\lambda^P(x,\cdot)\ll\nu
   \quad\forall x\in E.
   \]

2. **Instantaneous exceptional-set escape**
   \[
   P_t(x,N)=0
   \quad\forall x\in E,\ t>0,
   \]
   for the BZ exceptional set \(N\).

3. **Resolvent strong Feller**
   \[
   R_\lambda^P:L^\infty(E)\to C_b(E),
   \]
   or enough continuity to force \(R_\lambda{\bf 1}_B\equiv0\) whenever \(\nu(B)=0\).

4. **Uniform approximating-density bound**
   \[
   \sup_n\left\|\frac{dR_\lambda^n(x,\cdot)}{d\nu}\right\|_{L^p(\nu)}<\infty
   \]
   for some \(p>1\).

5. **Invariant-measure exceptional-set avoidance**
   \[
   \mu(N)=0
   \quad\text{for every invariant }\mu.
   \]

The most promising technical path seems to be (2) or (4): prove that ABLM solutions started from deterministic \(x\) do not hit BZ capacity-zero sets at positive times, perhaps via smooth approximants plus a capacity estimate whose constants depend only on the bounded potential \(F_n\), not on \(\|f_n'\|_\infty\). The bounded-potential comparison already gives uniform Hilbert–Schmidt bounds on average, but the missing step is a **pointwise-in-\(x\)** estimate.

## Bottom line

I **do not see a valid removal of the BZ exceptional set from the assumptions currently stated**. More importantly, the gap is not merely cosmetic: the BZ set \(N\) is an abstract capacity-zero set coming from quasi-regular Dirichlet-form potential theory, not an explicitly described “bad path regularity” set. A general theorem removing such a set from all starting points is false without an additional smoothing/strictness hypothesis.

What *would* complete the proof is a **capacity-smoothing estimate** for the ABLM transition laws or for the smooth approximants. I state it precisely below.

---

## 1. What the BZ exceptional set actually is

In BZ, the form is the closure of the gradient form
\[
\mathcal E(\varphi,\psi)=\frac12\int_H \langle\nabla\varphi,\nabla\psi\rangle\,d\nu,
\]
and capacity is defined from \(\mathcal E_1=\mathcal E+\langle\cdot,\cdot\rangle_{L^2(\nu)}\); a set is exceptional if its capacity is zero. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/1105.2779))

In Proposition 2.5, BZ obtain a Markov kernel only outside a capacity-zero set \(N\), using a general Dirichlet-form theorem; then the resolvent kernel is set artificially to zero on \(N\). The proof uses quasi-continuity on a nest, not an explicit description of \(N\). ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/1105.2779))

This matters: BZ later prove nonstationary path continuity by starting from \(x\in N^c\) and using the absolute-continuity property at positive time. That is, even their continuity/local-time verification is still q.e.; it does not identify \(N\) as merely “the set of initial profiles without local times.” ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/1105.2779))

So route (1) does **not** close the gap unless one proves the stronger fact that the ABLM law at every positive time avoids **all** BZ capacity-zero sets, or constructs a new explicit exceptional set known to be avoided.

---

## 2. No purely abstract q.e. theorem can solve this

A general theorem of the form

> “If a continuous Markov family is defined for every \(x\), agrees q.e. with a Dirichlet-form process, then it avoids the exceptional set from every starting point”

is false.

**Counterexample.** Let \(\gamma_3\) be the standard Gaussian measure on \(\mathbb R^3\), and let \(\mathcal E(f,f)=\frac12\int |\nabla f|^2\,d\gamma_3\), whose process is the 3D Ornstein–Uhlenbeck process. The singleton \(N=\{0\}\) has zero \(\mathcal E\)-capacity: take radial cutoffs equal to \(1\) on \(B_\varepsilon(0)\), \(0\) outside \(B_{2\varepsilon}(0)\); their \(L^2(\gamma_3)\)-mass is \(O(\varepsilon^3)\) and energy is \(O(\varepsilon)\).

The 3D OU process starting from \(x\ne0\) never hits \(0\). Define a new process by using the usual OU process for \(x\ne0\), but making \(0\) absorbing. This process has continuous paths, is Markov, and agrees with the OU Dirichlet-form process for every \(x\notin N\), hence q.e. But
\[
P_t(0,N)=1
\]
and \(\delta_0\) is an invariant probability measure supported on the exceptional set.

Thus the desired conclusion cannot follow from q.e. association, path continuity, and Markovianity alone. One needs an additional smoothing statement specific to the ABLM SPDE.

---

## 3. Why approximation by q.e. initial points is insufficient

Suppose \(x_m\in N^c\), \(x_m\to x\), and even suppose
\[
P_t(x_m,\cdot)\Rightarrow P_t(x,\cdot).
\]
This still does not imply \(P_t(x,N)=0\). Weak limits can create mass on null or capacity-zero sets; e.g. Gaussian laws \(N(0,1/m)\) are absolutely continuous but converge weakly to \(\delta_0\).

Likewise, the assumed ABLM stability of smooth approximants is too weak by itself. ABLM’s stability theorem gives convergence of smooth-drift solutions to the singular solution, but convergence in law alone does not prevent concentration on a capacity-zero set. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2011.13498))

---

## 4. A precise sufficient estimate that would close the gap

Let \(\operatorname{Cap}\) be the BZ capacity. Suppose \(P_t^n\) are the smooth approximating semigroups and
\[
P_t^n(x,\cdot)\Rightarrow P_t(x,\cdot)
\]
for every deterministic \(x\) and \(t>0\). The following estimate is sufficient:

\[
\boxed{
\forall x,\ t>0\quad \exists C_{x,t}<\infty,\ \alpha>0
\text{ such that }
\sup_n P_t^n(x,O)\le C_{x,t}\operatorname{Cap}(O)^\alpha
\quad\text{for every open }O.
}
\tag{CE}
\]

Indeed, if \(\operatorname{Cap}(N)=0\), choose open \(O_k\supset N\) with \(\operatorname{Cap}(O_k)\downarrow0\). By Portmanteau,
\[
P_t(x,O_k)\le \liminf_n P_t^n(x,O_k)
\le C_{x,t}\operatorname{Cap}(O_k)^\alpha.
\]
Letting \(k\to\infty\) gives
\[
P_t(x,N)=0.
\]

A more checkable sufficient condition is a uniform density bound: if for some \(p>1\),
\[
\sup_n \left\|\frac{dP_t^n(x,\cdot)}{d\nu}\right\|_{L^p(\nu)}<\infty,
\]
then
\[
P_t^n(x,O)\le C_{x,t}\nu(O)^{1-1/p}
\le C_{x,t}\operatorname{Cap}(O)^{1-1/p},
\]
because \(\nu(O)\le \operatorname{Cap}(O)\). Hence (CE) follows.

The bounded-potential comparison in BZ gives Hilbert–Schmidt bounds averaged over the invariant measure, but that is not pointwise in a deterministic starting state; an averaged \(L^2(\nu\otimes\nu)\) heat-kernel bound can still miss an exceptional starting point. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/1105.2779))

---

## 5. If (CE) holds, the uniqueness proof is complete

Once \(P_t(x,N)=0\) is known for every \(x\) and \(t>0\), the rest is short.

Let \(B\) be Borel with \(\nu(B)=0\). For \(y\notin N\), BZ/ABLM identification gives
\[
R_\lambda^P(y,B)=0.
\]
For arbitrary \(x\) and \(s>0\),
\[
R_\lambda^P1_B(x)
=
\int_0^s e^{-\lambda r}P_r1_B(x)\,dr
+
e^{-\lambda s}\int R_\lambda^P1_B(y)\,P_s(x,dy).
\]
The second term is zero because \(P_s(x,N)=0\); the first term is at most \(s\). Letting \(s\downarrow0\) gives
\[
R_\lambda^P(x,B)=0.
\]
Thus \(R_\lambda^P(x,\cdot)\ll\nu\) for every \(x\). Then the argument from t1 applies: every invariant probability is absolutely continuous with respect to \(\nu\), its density is an \(L^1\)-fixed point of the symmetric irreducible semigroup, and hence the density is \(1\). Therefore \(\nu\) is the unique invariant probability.

---

## 6. Weaker invariant-measure route

It would also suffice to prove only
\[
\mu(N)=0
\quad\text{for every invariant probability }\mu.
\]
Then for \(\nu(B)=0\),
\[
\mu(B)=\lambda\int R_\lambda^P(x,B)\,\mu(dx)=0,
\]
because \(R_\lambda^P(x,B)=0\) for \(x\notin N\). Hence \(\mu\ll\nu\), and t1 again gives \(\mu=\nu\).

But the same obstruction remains: stationarity alone does not prevent an invariant measure from living on a polar/exceptional set, as the absorbing-point OU example shows.

---

## Conclusion

The missing step is exactly a **pointwise capacity-smoothing estimate** or an equivalent result such as full resolvent strong Feller, full transition/resolvent absolute continuity, or uniform \(L^p\)-density bounds for the smooth approximants. The currently stated ABLM convergence and BZ q.e. identification do not by themselves supply that estimate.

[unverified self-report — verification not run or not yet complete | self_declared_is_relaxation: False | self_declared_problem_solved (the agent's own claim, NOT verified): Let $u(x,t)$ be  a real-valued  solution to the following stochastic partial differential equation (SPDE):
\begin{align}\tag{$*$}\label{maineq}
    \frac{\partial u}{\partial t}(x,t) &= \frac{1}{2} \frac{\partial^2 u}{\partial x^2}(x,t) + \delta_0(u(x,t)) +  \dot{W}(x,t) \quad\text{for }t \geq 0\\
    u(x,0) &= u_0(x)\in C_0([0,1])\nonumber\\
    u(0,t) &= u(1,t)=0\quad\text{for all }t\geq0    \nonumber
\end{align}
where $C_0([0,1]):=\{f\in C([0,1],\R): f(0)=f(1)=0\}$, $x \in [0,1]$, $t \geq 0$, $\delta_0$ is the Dirac delta
function at $0$, and $\dot{W}(x,t)$ is space-time white noise, $u_0\in C_0([0,1])$ is the initial condition. We understand
the probabilistically weak PDE mild solution of the equation in the
sense described in ``Well-posedness of stochastic heat equation with
distributional drift and skew stochastic heat equation'' by Siva
Athreya, Oleg Butkovsky, Khoa Lê, Leonid Mytnik (2024). 
We assume that the existence, uniqueness, and approximation results of [ABLM24] extend to the SPDE on $[0,1]$ with Dirichlet boundary conditions, i.e. with the Dirichlet heat kernel and initial data $u_0\in C_0([0,1])$. In
particular, we assume that there exists a unique (in law) adapted mild solution to \eqref{maineq} on $C([0,T],C_0([0,1]))$.


We further assume that if $u^n$ is a solution to this SPDE with the drift $G_{1/n}$ in place of $\delta_0$, where $G_{1/n}(x):=\sqrt{\frac{n}{2\pi}} \exp(-\frac{n x^2}{2})$ is the 1D heat kernel on $\mathbb{R}$ with variance $1/n$, then for any $t>0$ we have 
\begin{equation*}
u^n(t)\to u(t),\qquad\text{weakly, \,\, as $n\to\infty$}.
\end{equation*}

Let $P_t$ be the Markov semigroup associated with the solution $u(t)$, i.e., for any bounded measurable function $f\colon C_0([0,1])\to\R$,
\begin{align}
    P_t f(u_0) = \mathbb{E}[f(u(\cdot,t)) | u(\cdot,0) = u_0(\cdot)],\quad u_0\in C_0([0,1]).
\end{align}


Prove or disprove the following statement:  $P_t$ has at most one invariant probability measure $\mu$ on $C_0([0,1])$.]

## Verdict

I did **not** derive (A)–(D) from the currently stated hypotheses alone. What I can prove is a sharper reduction: **uniform finite-dimensional projected \(L^p\) density bounds imply (A), (B), and (D), hence uniqueness via the t1 argument.** This is a concrete target for Malliavin/PDE estimates.

I also found two important obstructions:

1. **Small last-time drift errors are not enough**: dense capacity-zero sets allow an anticipating perturbation of arbitrarily small sup-norm to concentrate a Gaussian on a null/capacity-zero set.
2. **Standard Malliavin estimates blow up**: quantities involving \(b_n'=G_{1/n}'\) diverge like powers of \(n\); exploiting cancellation would require genuinely new local-time derivative estimates.

Relevant source context: ABLM prove well-posedness for distributional drifts including \(\delta_0\), and smooth approximation convergence; BZ construct the Gibbs Dirichlet form and obtain only quasi-everywhere/exceptional-set absolute continuity, explicitly noting strong Feller is out of reach. ([archive.wias-berlin.de](https://archive.wias-berlin.de/servlets/MCRFileNodeServlet/wias_derivate_00003518/2011.13498.pdf))

---

## 1. A projective \(L^p\) criterion that would prove the desired smoothing

Let \(E=C_0([0,1])\), let \(\nu\) be the Gibbs measure from t1, and let
\[
\pi_m u=(\langle u,e_1\rangle,\dots,\langle u,e_m\rangle),\qquad 
\nu_m:=\nu\circ\pi_m^{-1}.
\]

### Theorem: projective density bound \(\Rightarrow\) full deterministic-start smoothing

Fix \(t>0\), compact \(K\subset E\), and \(p>1\). Suppose that for every \(n\), \(x\in K\), and \(m\ge1\),
\[
\pi_m\#P_t^n(x,\cdot)\ll \nu_m
\]
with density \(q_{m,n,x}\), and suppose
\[
\boxed{
\sup_{m\ge1}\sup_{n\ge1}\sup_{x\in K}
\|q_{m,n,x}\|_{L^p(\nu_m)}
\le C_{t,K,p}<\infty .
}
\tag{Proj-\(p\)}
\]
Then
\[
P_t^n(x,\cdot)\ll\nu
\]
for all \(n,x\in K\), with
\[
\sup_{n,x\in K}
\left\|\frac{dP_t^n(x,\cdot)}{d\nu}\right\|_{L^p(\nu)}
\le C_{t,K,p}.
\]
Consequently, with \(\alpha=1-\frac1p\),
\[
\boxed{
\sup_{n}\sup_{x\in K}P_t^n(x,A)
\le C_{t,K,p}\,\nu(A)^\alpha
}
\tag{A'}
\]
for every Borel \(A\subset E\). This implies the compact-local uniform absolute continuity (A).

Moreover, since \(\nu(O)\le \operatorname{Cap}(O)\) for open \(O\),
\[
\boxed{
\sup_n\sup_{x\in K}P_t^n(x,O)
\le C_{t,K,p}\operatorname{Cap}(O)^\alpha .
}
\tag{B'}
\]
So (Proj-\(p\)) also gives capacity smoothing.

Finally, if \(P_t^n(x,\cdot)\Rightarrow P_t(x,\cdot)\), then the same \(L^p\) bound passes to \(P_t(x,\cdot)\). In particular, for each deterministic \(x\),
\[
P_t(x,\cdot)\ll\nu,
\]
which is (D).

### Proof

Let \(q=p/(p-1)\). For any bounded \(\pi_m\)-measurable test function \(\varphi=f\circ\pi_m\),
\[
\left|\int_E\varphi\,dP_t^n(x,\cdot)\right|
=
\left|\int_{\mathbb R^m} f(y)q_{m,n,x}(y)\,\nu_m(dy)\right|
\le
C_{t,K,p}\|\varphi\|_{L^q(\nu)}.
\]
Cylinder functions are dense in \(L^q(E,\nu)\), because the Fourier coordinates generate the Borel \(\sigma\)-field of \(E\). Hence \(P_t^n(x,\cdot)\) extends to a bounded functional on \(L^q(\nu)\). By duality, it has an \(L^p(\nu)\) density with norm at most \(C_{t,K,p}\).

Hölder then gives
\[
P_t^n(x,A)
\le
C_{t,K,p}\nu(A)^{1-1/p}.
\]
For open \(O\), the capacity definition gives \(\nu(O)\le\operatorname{Cap}(O)\), so the capacity estimate follows. If \(P_t^n(x,\cdot)\Rightarrow P_t(x,\cdot)\), the same bound passes to the weak limit by testing against bounded continuous cylinder functions and repeating the duality argument.

Thus (Proj-\(p\)) is a sufficient deterministic-start smoothing theorem.

---

## 2. Why this criterion is useful

The condition (Proj-\(p\)) is finite-dimensional:

\[
\sup_{m,n,x\in K}
\left\|
\frac{d\,\mathcal L(\langle u_t^n,e_1\rangle,\dots,\langle u_t^n,e_m\rangle)}
     {d\nu_m}
\right\|_{L^p(\nu_m)}
<\infty .
\]

Since \(d\nu/d\gamma\) is bounded above and below, \(d\nu_m/d\gamma_m\) is also bounded above and below uniformly in \(m\). So one may equivalently prove the estimate relative to the \(m\)-dimensional Gaussian projection \(\gamma_m\).

This is the cleanest Malliavin target: one does **not** need a pointwise full infinite-dimensional density estimate at the start; one needs uniformly \(L^p\)-controlled finite-dimensional projection densities.

If this estimate is proved for every compact \(K\) and one fixed \(t>0\), then t3 gives \(\mu\ll\nu\) for every invariant measure \(\mu\). Then t1’s irreducible symmetric Dirichlet-form argument gives \(\mu=\nu\).

---

## 3. Why standard Malliavin estimates blow up

For the smooth approximants,
\[
b_n(r)=G_{1/n}(r),\qquad 
b_n'(r)=-nrG_{1/n}(r).
\]
The formal Malliavin derivative satisfies a random linear parabolic equation
\[
\partial_s J
=
\frac12\partial_{xx}J+b_n'(u_s^n)J.
\]
Any estimate using \(\sup b_n'\), \(\int |b_n'(u_s^n)|\,ds\), or \(\int |b_n'(u_s^n)|^2\,ds\) degenerates.

Indeed,
\[
\sup_r b_n'(r)=\frac{n}{\sqrt{2\pi e}},
\]
and if \(X\sim N(0,\sigma^2)\) with \(\sigma>0\), then
\[
\mathbb E|b_n'(X)|\asymp \sqrt n,
\qquad
\mathbb E|b_n'(X)|^2\asymp n^{3/2}.
\]
Since \(\nu_n\) is uniformly equivalent to the Brownian-bridge law, these divergences persist under stationary-size laws. Thus the usual Malliavin route can only work if it exploits a cancellation in the signed potential \(b_n'\), not merely its size. That cancellation would amount to controlling a derivative of local time at zero, which is much stronger than the ABLM \(3/4\)-order drift increment estimates.

---

## 4. Heat-kernel comparison: what would suffice and why bounded potentials alone do not give it

A strict diagonal estimate would immediately imply (A):
\[
\sup_n\sup_{x\in K}
\int p_t^n(x,y)^2\,\nu_n(dy)<\infty .
\tag{Diag}
\]
Then
\[
P_t^n(x,A)
\le
\|p_t^n(x,\cdot)\|_{L^2(\nu_n)}\,\nu_n(A)^{1/2}
\lesssim \nu(A)^{1/2}.
\]

BZ prove a Hilbert–Schmidt kernel only \(\nu\)-a.e.; their Proposition 2.5 obtains a resolvent kernel outside a capacity-zero set and then sets the kernel to zero on the exceptional set. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/1105.2779)) That is exactly insufficient for deterministic starting points.

The boundedness of the Gibbs potential gives uniform equivalence \(\nu_n\sim\gamma\), and hence averaged Hilbert–Schmidt estimates, but not pointwise diagonal control. A true heat-kernel comparison of the form
\[
p_t^n(x,x)\le C_{t,K}p_{ct}^{OU}(x,x)
\]
would solve the problem. I do not see how to get such a comparison from bounded density perturbation alone in infinite dimension.

---

## 5. Capacity-zero sets can be dense; small anticipating shifts are dangerous

This is the main obstruction to the last-time decomposition
\[
u_t^n=S_\varepsilon u_{t-\varepsilon}^n+Z_\varepsilon+R_\varepsilon^n.
\]
Even if one proves
\[
\|R_\varepsilon^n\|_\infty\le \varepsilon
\]
with very high probability, that does **not** imply absolute continuity.

### Lemma: there are dense capacity-zero sets

For the Brownian-bridge Dirichlet form, every point of \(E\) has zero capacity. Indeed, project onto the first three Fourier coordinates. The capacity of a point in the three-dimensional Gaussian Sobolev space is zero, and pulling back test functions gives zero capacity for the corresponding cylinder, hence for the point. Since \(\nu\) and \(\gamma\) have comparable bounded densities, the same holds for \(\nu\). Therefore every countable dense subset \(D\subset E\) has \(\operatorname{Cap}(D)=0\).

### Anticipating small-shift counterexample

Let \(Z\) be a full-support Gaussian random element of \(E\), and fix \(\varepsilon>0\). Since \(D\) is dense, one can choose a measurable map \(T_\varepsilon:E\to D\) such that
\[
\|T_\varepsilon(z)-z\|_\infty<\varepsilon .
\]
Then
\[
Z+R_\varepsilon:=T_\varepsilon(Z),
\qquad
R_\varepsilon=T_\varepsilon(Z)-Z,
\]
satisfies \(\|R_\varepsilon\|_\infty<\varepsilon\), but the law of \(Z+R_\varepsilon\) is supported on the capacity-zero set \(D\).

So a last-noise argument needs more than smallness of \(R_\varepsilon^n\). It needs a **nonanticipative or Ramer-type nonsingularity estimate**, for example uniform control of the Malliavin derivative of the map
\[
Z_\varepsilon\mapsto Z_\varepsilon+R_\varepsilon^n.
\]
The blow-up of \(b_n'\) is precisely the obstruction to proving such a Ramer estimate uniformly.

---

## 6. Scale/skew transform: why it does not currently close the gap

For the one-dimensional SDE with a delta drift, a scale transform removes the local-time drift. For the approximants, one would try
\[
s_n'(r)=\exp\!\left(-2\int_{-\infty}^r b_n(a)\,da\right),
\]
so that
\[
\frac12s_n''(r)+b_n(r)s_n'(r)=0.
\]
For an SDE this works.

For the SPDE, however, \(v=s_n(u)\) formally produces terms such as
\[
s_n''(u)(\partial_x u)^2
\]
and an infinite Itô correction coming from space-time white noise. ABLM explicitly note that Zvonkin-type transforms are not useful in this SPDE setting because the Itô formula involves renormalized nonlinear terms. ([archive.wias-berlin.de](https://archive.wias-berlin.de/servlets/MCRFileNodeServlet/wias_derivate_00003518/2011.13498.pdf))

Thus the transform does not presently yield a clean uniformly elliptic multiplicative-noise stochastic heat equation. Making this route work would require a renormalized geometric transformation theorem plus a proof that the transformed equation has deterministic-start absolute continuity.

---

## 7. The sharp remaining estimate

The most concrete sufficient estimate is:

\[
\boxed{
\forall t>0,\ K\Subset E,\ \exists p>1,\ C_{t,K}<\infty
\text{ such that }
\sup_{m,n,x\in K}
\left\|
\frac{d\,\pi_m\#P_t^n(x,\cdot)}{d\nu_m}
\right\|_{L^p(\nu_m)}
\le C_{t,K}.
}
\]

This finite-dimensional projected estimate implies:

- compact-local uniform absolute continuity (A),
- capacity smoothing (B),
- \(P_t(x,N)=0\) for every capacity-zero BZ exceptional set \(N\),
- full transition absolute continuity (D),
- and then uniqueness of invariant measures via the t1 theorem.

At present, the main technical barrier is proving this estimate uniformly in both \(n\) and the projection dimension \(m\).

We need prove at most one invariant probability measure for the Dirichlet ABLM solution of the stochastic heat equation with drift delta_0(u). Do not rederive the Gibbs sign or BZ irreducibility unless needed. Your task is to attack the Doob/t0-regularity route. Classical smooth-gradient additive stochastic heat equations prove uniqueness by showing that for every t>0 and every initial states x,y, P_t(x,.) and P_t(y,.) are mutually equivalent; then Doob/Stettner-Seidler gives uniqueness of the invariant measure. In the smooth proof, nonlinear laws are compared to the linear OU law by Girsanov for bounded adapted drifts, but this fails naively for the delta drift. Investigate whether fixed-time t0-regularity can nevertheless be proved for the singular ABLM/BZ skew heat equation. Concrete subtasks: (1) state a precise theorem reducing uniqueness to mutual equivalence of positive-time transition laws, including the needed stochastic continuity/Feller hypotheses; (2) try to prove P_t(x,.) is equivalent to the linear OU transition law or to the Gibbs measure nu for every deterministic x, using any of BZ strict association, finite-dimensional Galerkin BV-potential forms, scale/skew transforms, Malliavin fixed-time arguments, or approximation; (3) if full equivalence cannot be proved, identify exactly where each attempted extension of the smooth proof fails and whether the obstruction is path-law singularity only or also fixed-time singularity; (4) extract any rigorous conditional lemma weaker than t6's full Lp criterion that would still imply uniqueness. Use web search for precise literature if useful. Return proven lemmas, failed attempts, and the sharp remaining estimate.

where $C_0([0,1]):=\{f\in C([0,1],\R): f(0)=f(1)=0\}$, $x \in [0,1]$, $t \geq 0$, $\delta_0$ is the Dirac delta
function at $0$, and $\dot{W}(x,t)$ is space-time white noise, $u_0\in C_0([0,1])$ is the initial condition. We understand
the probabilistically weak PDE mild solution of the equation in the
sense described in ``Well-posedness of stochastic heat equation with
distributional drift and skew stochastic heat equation'' by Siva
Athreya, Oleg Butkovsky, Khoa Lê, Leonid Mytnik (2024). 
We assume that the existence, uniqueness, and approximation results of [ABLM24] extend to the SPDE on $[0,1]$ with Dirichlet boundary conditions, i.e. with the Dirichlet heat kernel and initial data $u_0\in C_0([0,1])$. In
particular, we assume that there exists a unique (in law) adapted mild solution to \eqref{maineq} on $C([0,T],C_0([0,1]))$.


We further assume that if $u^n$ is a solution to this SPDE with the drift $G_{1/n}$ in place of $\delta_0$, where $G_{1/n}(x):=\sqrt{\frac{n}{2\pi}} \exp(-\frac{n x^2}{2})$ is the 1D heat kernel on $\mathbb{R}$ with variance $1/n$, then for any $t>0$ we have 
\begin{equation*}
u^n(t)\to u(t),\qquad\text{weakly, \,\, as $n\to\infty$}.
\end{equation*}

Let $P_t$ be the Markov semigroup associated with the solution $u(t)$, i.e., for any bounded measurable function $f\colon C_0([0,1])\to\R$,
\begin{align}
    P_t f(u_0) = \mathbb{E}[f(u(\cdot,t)) | u(\cdot,0) = u_0(\cdot)],\quad u_0\in C_0([0,1]).
\end{align}


Prove or disprove the following statement:  $P_t$ has at most one invariant probability measure $\mu$ on $C_0([0,1])$.]
Source: task_outputs.jsonl — raw agent output. The values in the metadata line above are self-declared and have not been checked. Treat the proof as a draft, not as established.
## Verdict

I did **not** derive (A)–(D) from the currently stated hypotheses alone. What I can prove is a sharper reduction: **uniform finite-dimensional projected \(L^p\) density bounds imply (A), (B), and (D), hence uniqueness via the t1 argument.** This is a concrete target for Malliavin/PDE estimates.

I also found two important obstructions:

1. **Small last-time drift errors are not enough**: dense capacity-zero sets allow an anticipating perturbation of arbitrarily small sup-norm to concentrate a Gaussian on a null/capacity-zero set.
2. **Standard Malliavin estimates blow up**: quantities involving \(b_n'=G_{1/n}'\) diverge like powers of \(n\); exploiting cancellation would require genuinely new local-time derivative estimates.

Relevant source context: ABLM prove well-posedness for distributional drifts including \(\delta_0\), and smooth approximation convergence; BZ construct the Gibbs Dirichlet form and obtain only quasi-everywhere/exceptional-set absolute continuity, explicitly noting strong Feller is out of reach. ([archive.wias-berlin.de](https://archive.wias-berlin.de/servlets/MCRFileNodeServlet/wias_derivate_00003518/2011.13498.pdf))

---

## 1. A projective \(L^p\) criterion that would prove the desired smoothing

Let \(E=C_0([0,1])\), let \(\nu\) be the Gibbs measure from t1, and let
\[
\pi_m u=(\langle u,e_1\rangle,\dots,\langle u,e_m\rangle),\qquad 
\nu_m:=\nu\circ\pi_m^{-1}.
\]

### Theorem: projective density bound \(\Rightarrow\) full deterministic-start smoothing

Fix \(t>0\), compact \(K\subset E\), and \(p>1\). Suppose that for every \(n\), \(x\in K\), and \(m\ge1\),
\[
\pi_m\#P_t^n(x,\cdot)\ll \nu_m
\]
with density \(q_{m,n,x}\), and suppose
\[
\boxed{
\sup_{m\ge1}\sup_{n\ge1}\sup_{x\in K}
\|q_{m,n,x}\|_{L^p(\nu_m)}
\le C_{t,K,p}<\infty .
}
\tag{Proj-\(p\)}
\]
Then
\[
P_t^n(x,\cdot)\ll\nu
\]
for all \(n,x\in K\), with
\[
\sup_{n,x\in K}
\left\|\frac{dP_t^n(x,\cdot)}{d\nu}\right\|_{L^p(\nu)}
\le C_{t,K,p}.
\]
Consequently, with \(\alpha=1-\frac1p\),
\[
\boxed{
\sup_{n}\sup_{x\in K}P_t^n(x,A)
\le C_{t,K,p}\,\nu(A)^\alpha
}
\tag{A'}
\]
for every Borel \(A\subset E\). This implies the compact-local uniform absolute continuity (A).

Moreover, since \(\nu(O)\le \operatorname{Cap}(O)\) for open \(O\),
\[
\boxed{
\sup_n\sup_{x\in K}P_t^n(x,O)
\le C_{t,K,p}\operatorname{Cap}(O)^\alpha .
}
\tag{B'}
\]
So (Proj-\(p\)) also gives capacity smoothing.

Finally, if \(P_t^n(x,\cdot)\Rightarrow P_t(x,\cdot)\), then the same \(L^p\) bound passes to \(P_t(x,\cdot)\). In particular, for each deterministic \(x\),
\[
P_t(x,\cdot)\ll\nu,
\]
which is (D).

### Proof

Let \(q=p/(p-1)\). For any bounded \(\pi_m\)-measurable test function \(\varphi=f\circ\pi_m\),
\[
\left|\int_E\varphi\,dP_t^n(x,\cdot)\right|
=
\left|\int_{\mathbb R^m} f(y)q_{m,n,x}(y)\,\nu_m(dy)\right|
\le
C_{t,K,p}\|\varphi\|_{L^q(\nu)}.
\]
Cylinder functions are dense in \(L^q(E,\nu)\), because the Fourier coordinates generate the Borel \(\sigma\)-field of \(E\). Hence \(P_t^n(x,\cdot)\) extends to a bounded functional on \(L^q(\nu)\). By duality, it has an \(L^p(\nu)\) density with norm at most \(C_{t,K,p}\).

Hölder then gives
\[
P_t^n(x,A)
\le
C_{t,K,p}\nu(A)^{1-1/p}.
\]
For open \(O\), the capacity definition gives \(\nu(O)\le\operatorname{Cap}(O)\), so the capacity estimate follows. If \(P_t^n(x,\cdot)\Rightarrow P_t(x,\cdot)\), the same bound passes to the weak limit by testing against bounded continuous cylinder functions and repeating the duality argument.

Thus (Proj-\(p\)) is a sufficient deterministic-start smoothing theorem.

---

## 2. Why this criterion is useful

The condition (Proj-\(p\)) is finite-dimensional:

\[
\sup_{m,n,x\in K}
\left\|
\frac{d\,\mathcal L(\langle u_t^n,e_1\rangle,\dots,\langle u_t^n,e_m\rangle)}
     {d\nu_m}
\right\|_{L^p(\nu_m)}
<\infty .
\]

Since \(d\nu/d\gamma\) is bounded above and below, \(d\nu_m/d\gamma_m\) is also bounded above and below uniformly in \(m\). So one may equivalently prove the estimate relative to the \(m\)-dimensional Gaussian projection \(\gamma_m\).

This is the cleanest Malliavin target: one does **not** need a pointwise full infinite-dimensional density estimate at the start; one needs uniformly \(L^p\)-controlled finite-dimensional projection densities.

If this estimate is proved for every compact \(K\) and one fixed \(t>0\), then t3 gives \(\mu\ll\nu\) for every invariant measure \(\mu\). Then t1’s irreducible symmetric Dirichlet-form argument gives \(\mu=\nu\).

---

## 3. Why standard Malliavin estimates blow up

For the smooth approximants,
\[
b_n(r)=G_{1/n}(r),\qquad 
b_n'(r)=-nrG_{1/n}(r).
\]
The formal Malliavin derivative satisfies a random linear parabolic equation
\[
\partial_s J
=
\frac12\partial_{xx}J+b_n'(u_s^n)J.
\]
Any estimate using \(\sup b_n'\), \(\int |b_n'(u_s^n)|\,ds\), or \(\int |b_n'(u_s^n)|^2\,ds\) degenerates.

Indeed,
\[
\sup_r b_n'(r)=\frac{n}{\sqrt{2\pi e}},
\]
and if \(X\sim N(0,\sigma^2)\) with \(\sigma>0\), then
\[
\mathbb E|b_n'(X)|\asymp \sqrt n,
\qquad
\mathbb E|b_n'(X)|^2\asymp n^{3/2}.
\]
Since \(\nu_n\) is uniformly equivalent to the Brownian-bridge law, these divergences persist under stationary-size laws. Thus the usual Malliavin route can only work if it exploits a cancellation in the signed potential \(b_n'\), not merely its size. That cancellation would amount to controlling a derivative of local time at zero, which is much stronger than the ABLM \(3/4\)-order drift increment estimates.

---

## 4. Heat-kernel comparison: what would suffice and why bounded potentials alone do not give it

A strict diagonal estimate would immediately imply (A):
\[
\sup_n\sup_{x\in K}
\int p_t^n(x,y)^2\,\nu_n(dy)<\infty .
\tag{Diag}
\]
Then
\[
P_t^n(x,A)
\le
\|p_t^n(x,\cdot)\|_{L^2(\nu_n)}\,\nu_n(A)^{1/2}
\lesssim \nu(A)^{1/2}.
\]

BZ prove a Hilbert–Schmidt kernel only \(\nu\)-a.e.; their Proposition 2.5 obtains a resolvent kernel outside a capacity-zero set and then sets the kernel to zero on the exceptional set. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/1105.2779)) That is exactly insufficient for deterministic starting points.

The boundedness of the Gibbs potential gives uniform equivalence \(\nu_n\sim\gamma\), and hence averaged Hilbert–Schmidt estimates, but not pointwise diagonal control. A true heat-kernel comparison of the form
\[
p_t^n(x,x)\le C_{t,K}p_{ct}^{OU}(x,x)
\]
would solve the problem. I do not see how to get such a comparison from bounded density perturbation alone in infinite dimension.

---

## 5. Capacity-zero sets can be dense; small anticipating shifts are dangerous

This is the main obstruction to the last-time decomposition
\[
u_t^n=S_\varepsilon u_{t-\varepsilon}^n+Z_\varepsilon+R_\varepsilon^n.
\]
Even if one proves
\[
\|R_\varepsilon^n\|_\infty\le \varepsilon
\]
with very high probability, that does **not** imply absolute continuity.

### Lemma: there are dense capacity-zero sets

For the Brownian-bridge Dirichlet form, every point of \(E\) has zero capacity. Indeed, project onto the first three Fourier coordinates. The capacity of a point in the three-dimensional Gaussian Sobolev space is zero, and pulling back test functions gives zero capacity for the corresponding cylinder, hence for the point. Since \(\nu\) and \(\gamma\) have comparable bounded densities, the same holds for \(\nu\). Therefore every countable dense subset \(D\subset E\) has \(\operatorname{Cap}(D)=0\).

### Anticipating small-shift counterexample

Let \(Z\) be a full-support Gaussian random element of \(E\), and fix \(\varepsilon>0\). Since \(D\) is dense, one can choose a measurable map \(T_\varepsilon:E\to D\) such that
\[
\|T_\varepsilon(z)-z\|_\infty<\varepsilon .
\]
Then
\[
Z+R_\varepsilon:=T_\varepsilon(Z),
\qquad
R_\varepsilon=T_\varepsilon(Z)-Z,
\]
satisfies \(\|R_\varepsilon\|_\infty<\varepsilon\), but the law of \(Z+R_\varepsilon\) is supported on the capacity-zero set \(D\).

So a last-noise argument needs more than smallness of \(R_\varepsilon^n\). It needs a **nonanticipative or Ramer-type nonsingularity estimate**, for example uniform control of the Malliavin derivative of the map
\[
Z_\varepsilon\mapsto Z_\varepsilon+R_\varepsilon^n.
\]
The blow-up of \(b_n'\) is precisely the obstruction to proving such a Ramer estimate uniformly.

---

## 6. Scale/skew transform: why it does not currently close the gap

For the one-dimensional SDE with a delta drift, a scale transform removes the local-time drift. For the approximants, one would try
\[
s_n'(r)=\exp\!\left(-2\int_{-\infty}^r b_n(a)\,da\right),
\]
so that
\[
\frac12s_n''(r)+b_n(r)s_n'(r)=0.
\]
For an SDE this works.

For the SPDE, however, \(v=s_n(u)\) formally produces terms such as
\[
s_n''(u)(\partial_x u)^2
\]
and an infinite Itô correction coming from space-time white noise. ABLM explicitly note that Zvonkin-type transforms are not useful in this SPDE setting because the Itô formula involves renormalized nonlinear terms. ([archive.wias-berlin.de](https://archive.wias-berlin.de/servlets/MCRFileNodeServlet/wias_derivate_00003518/2011.13498.pdf))

Thus the transform does not presently yield a clean uniformly elliptic multiplicative-noise stochastic heat equation. Making this route work would require a renormalized geometric transformation theorem plus a proof that the transformed equation has deterministic-start absolute continuity.

---

## 7. The sharp remaining estimate

The most concrete sufficient estimate is:

\[
\boxed{
\forall t>0,\ K\Subset E,\ \exists p>1,\ C_{t,K}<\infty
\text{ such that }
\sup_{m,n,x\in K}
\left\|
\frac{d\,\pi_m\#P_t^n(x,\cdot)}{d\nu_m}
\right\|_{L^p(\nu_m)}
\le C_{t,K}.
}
\]

This finite-dimensional projected estimate implies:

- compact-local uniform absolute continuity (A),
- capacity smoothing (B),
- \(P_t(x,N)=0\) for every capacity-zero BZ exceptional set \(N\),
- full transition absolute continuity (D),
- and then uniqueness of invariant measures via the t1 theorem.

At present, the main technical barrier is proving this estimate uniformly in both \(n\) and the projection dimension \(m\).

Build directly on t6's projected Lp criterion. For the smooth approximants u^n with b_n=G_{1/n}, fix the Dirichlet eigenbasis e_i and define Y_i^n(t)=<u_t^n,e_i>. Then Y_i^n satisfies dY_i = -lambda_i Y_i dt/2 + B_i^n(t) dt + d beta_i(t), where B_i^n(t)=int_0^1 e_i(x) G_{1/n}(u_t^n(x)) dx. Full H-valued Girsanov fails because ||G_{1/n}(u)||_H diverges, but each projected B_h^n should converge to a spatial local-time functional. Your task is to prove or disprove useful uniform-in-n estimates for these projected drifts from deterministic initial data in compact subsets of C_0([0,1]). Concrete goals: (1) prove polynomial or exponential moment bounds for int_0^T |B_h^n(t)|^2 dt, uniformly in n, for smooth h; (2) use such bounds in a finite-dimensional Girsanov or density argument to show that pi_m#P_t^n(x,.) has an Lp density relative to the m-dimensional OU or Gibbs marginal, uniformly in n for fixed m; (3) track the dependence on m carefully and see whether it can be made dimension-free, summable in eigenmodes, or otherwise sufficient to imply full absolute continuity of invariant measures; (4) if the estimates fail, give the exact scaling counterestimate. You may use ABLM stochastic sewing/local nondeterminism estimates, occupation density heuristics, or new arguments, but be explicit about every bound and constant dependence.

writeup_tasks.statement: Conditional uniqueness from compact-local uniform absolute continuity of the smooth approximating semigroups.
writeup_tasks.writeup_agent_prompt: Write a fully self-contained mathematical note proving the following conditional theorem. Define E=C_0([0,1]) with the sup norm. Let P_t be a Markov semigroup on E, let P_t^n be approximating Markov semigroups, and let nu be a probability measure on E. Assume: (i) for every bounded continuous f and every x in E, P_t^n f(x) tends to P_t f(x) for a fixed t=t0>0; (ii) compact-local uniform absolute continuity holds: for every compact K subset E and every epsilon>0 there is delta>0 such that nu(A)<delta implies sup_n sup_{x in K} P_t0^n(x,A)<epsilon for all Borel A; (iii) every P_t-invariant probability measure that is absolutely continuous with respect to nu is equal to nu. Prove that P_t has at most one invariant probability measure, namely nu if it is invariant. Also prove the useful sufficient condition that a compact-local uniform Lp density bound for P_t0^n(x,.) with respect to nu implies assumption (ii). The proof must be self-contained: state all hypotheses, define all notation, prove the tightness/compact K step, use Portmanteau correctly for open neighborhoods of nu-null sets, and then invoke assumption (iii). Include a short optional paragraph explaining how assumption (iii) is supplied in the skew heat equation by the symmetric irreducible Gibbs Dirichlet-form argument, but keep the main theorem conditional. Do not refer to previous tasks or to this conversation.

plan.reasoning: The new t7/t8 results rule out the most naive density route: for each smooth mollifier the transition law is equivalent to the OU/Gibbs reference law, but the Girsanov constants blow up, and finite-dimensional projected Girsanov estimates lose like sqrt(m). However, this sqrt(m) loss may still be compatible with asymptotic coupling: to couple within a fixed epsilon one can choose a finite m, pay a finite low-mode control cost, and make the uncontrolled high-mode tail small by heat smoothing. This is now the highest-value direction. In parallel, the last-noise/Cameron-Martin smoothing idea deserves one focused attack: the terminal drift convolution is much smoother than the instantaneous local-time drift, and if one can combine this with a triangular/Ramer/Girsanov argument, it would give pointwise fixed-time absolute continuity. The web search did not reveal a ready-made strong-Feller theorem for the skew stochastic heat equation; it mainly reconfirmed the BZ Dirichlet-form/Gibbs setup and the ABLM well-posedness context. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/abs/1105.2779))
plan.strategic_note: Do not keep trying to prove dimension-free Lp bounds for projected densities; t8 shows the natural energy scale is sqrt(m). Pivot to methods where m may depend on the target accuracy: low-mode controlled generalized/asymptotic coupling is the most promising. The backup route is fixed-time last-noise quasi-invariance, but it must overcome the known danger that anticipating Cameron-Martin shifts can concentrate Gaussian laws on null sets.

new_proven_results.statement: A skeleton or normalized-resolvent kernel with pairwise non-singular transition probabilities for all initial states implies at most one invariant probability measure for the semigroup.
new_proven_results.sketch: Any invariant probability for the continuous semigroup is invariant for the skeleton or normalized resolvent. The discrete Doob/non-singularity argument rules out two mutually singular ergodic invariant components, and ergodic decomposition gives uniqueness.

new_proven_results.statement: For the linear Dirichlet OU stochastic heat equation, every positive-time transition law from a deterministic initial condition is equivalent to the Brownian-bridge law and hence to the bounded Gibbs perturbation ν.
new_proven_results.sketch: In sine modes, the transition covariance eigenvalues are (1-exp(-π²k²t))/(π²k²), equivalent to the Brownian-bridge covariance eigenvalues 1/(π²k²), and the deterministic heat-evolved mean is in the Cameron-Martin space.

new_proven_results.statement: For each bounded smooth drift b, the additive stochastic heat equation has positive-time transition laws equivalent to the OU transition laws.
new_proven_results.sketch: The drift B(u)(x)=b(u(x)) is L2-valued with path energy bounded by T ||b||∞², so Girsanov gives path-law equivalence with the linear OU equation and therefore fixed-time equivalence.

new_proven_results.statement: For the mollified delta drifts b_n=G_{1/n}, each fixed finite-dimensional projected local-time drift has uniform-in-n moment and small exponential-moment bounds, but the first m modes have sharp energy scale sqrt(m).
new_proven_results.sketch: Local nondeterminism and Berman-type local-time moment estimates bound scalar projections uniformly. Summing the covariance over the first m sine modes gives ∑_{i≤m} i^{-1/2} ≍ sqrt(m), and this scaling is sharp under the Brownian-bridge/Gibbs reference law.

new_proven_results.statement: For each fixed m, finite-dimensional Girsanov yields uniform-in-n absolute continuity of the first m projected transition law, with an L^{1+c/(1+sqrt(m))} density bound.
new_proven_results.sketch: The projected drift energy controls the finite-dimensional Girsanov density. The exponential-moment parameter is of order 1/(1+sqrt(m)), yielding an exponent above 1 for each fixed m but tending to 1 as m grows.

new_failed_attempts.approach: Uniform full-path Girsanov for b_n=G_{1/n}.
new_failed_attempts.reason: The full H=L2 drift energy diverges like sqrt(n), so Novikov, entropy, and reverse-Hölder constants degenerate.

new_failed_attempts.approach: Dimension-free projective Lp estimates from finite-dimensional Girsanov.
new_failed_attempts.reason: The first m projected local-time drift energy is sharply of order sqrt(m), so the best Girsanov exponent tends to 1 as m→∞.

new_failed_attempts.approach: Use finite-dimensional projected absolute continuity alone to infer full infinite-dimensional absolute continuity.
new_failed_attempts.reason: Without a uniform projective overlap or uniform integrability bound, finite-dimensional equivalence can still converge to mutually singular infinite-dimensional laws.

new_bottlenecks: Convert finite-m projected drift estimates into a genuine all-start uniqueness mechanism, preferably generalized/asymptotic coupling.
Prove uniform high-mode tail bounds for the heat-convolved singular drift in a norm strong enough to make low-mode coupling close in C0.
Handle Girsanov for controlled equations without circular moment estimates for the controlled local-time drift.
If using last-noise quasi-invariance, overcome the fact that random anticipating Cameron-Martin shifts can be singular without extra triangular/Ramer structure.
kb_updates.frontier: Establish pairwise non-singularity or generalized asymptotic coupling for the singular semigroup, using finite-dimensional low-mode controls plus high-mode heat smoothing rather than dimension-free density bounds.

task_assignments.description: Low-mode controlled generalized/asymptotic coupling from projected local-time estimates
task_assignments.solver_prompt:
You are attacking the main remaining bottleneck for uniqueness of the invariant measure of the Dirichlet stochastic heat equation with drift delta_0(u). Read the attached t7 and t8 outputs carefully.

Goal: Try to turn the finite-dimensional projected drift estimates from t8 into a Hairer-Mattingly-Scheutzow/Kulik-Scheutzow type generalized or asymptotic coupling proving that two invariant measures cannot be mutually singular. You may work first with the smooth approximating equations with b_n=G_{1/n}, then pass n→∞ using the assumed ABLM stability.

Suggested construction to analyze rigorously:
1. Let u^n solve du = A u dt + b_n(u) dt + dW from initial x, where A=(1/2)Delta with Dirichlet boundary.
2. Let v^n solve the controlled equation from y with the same W and an added low-mode control h^n_{m,lambda}(t) dt in the first m sine modes. Choose the sign so that for w=u^n-v^n, the first m modes satisfy approximately d Pi_m w = A Pi_m w dt - lambda Pi_m w dt. A natural candidate is h = Pi_m(b_n(u)-b_n(v)) + lambda Pi_m(u-v), added to the v equation.
3. Then the high modes satisfy Q_m w(T) = S_T Q_m(x-y) + integral_0^T S_{T-s} Q_m(b_n(u_s)-b_n(v_s)) ds. Try to bound this in C0 by choosing T large and then m large, using the H^{-s} nature of the local-time drift for s>1/4.
4. Use the t8 estimates to control the energy integral integral_0^T |h_s|^2 ds for each fixed m. It is acceptable if the cost grows like exp(C sqrt(m)); for coupling within a fixed epsilon, m may depend on epsilon.
5. Use a stopped/localized Girsanov argument: under the original measure, u has the correct law from x and v has a law absolutely continuous with respect to the law from y. This gives a generalized coupling if the event of successful closeness has positive probability.

Deliverables:
A. State a precise uniqueness criterion based on generalized/asymptotic coupling that would apply here. Include exact hypotheses needed: exact first marginal and second marginal absolutely continuous, positive probability of liminf distance 0 or distance below epsilon along a sequence, etc.
B. Carry out the controlled-coupling proof as far as possible. Be explicit about estimates for low-mode control energy and high-mode tail smoothing.
C. Identify and try to close the circularity problem: t8 estimates are for uncontrolled equations, whereas v is controlled. See whether stopping, equivalence of measures, or a priori deterministic bounds are enough.
D. If you can prove the needed generalized asymptotic coupling, state clearly that this proves at most one invariant probability for the original singular semigroup. If a gap remains, isolate the smallest missing estimate in a theorem-like form.

Do not spend time re-proving the Gibbs invariant measure or BZ irreducibility. The point is to exploit finite-m estimates despite their sqrt(m) growth.

## 1. Doob reduction: what fixed-time regularity would buy

Let \(E=C_0([0,1])\) with its Borel sigma-field. For uniqueness of invariant probabilities, one does **not** need Feller or strong Feller once a regularity condition is known.

**Doob/Stettner-Seidler skeleton criterion.** Let \(Q\) be either a skeleton kernel \(Q=P_{t_0}\), \(t_0>0\), or a normalized resolvent
\[
Q=\bar R_\lambda:=\lambda\int_0^\infty e^{-\lambda t}P_t\,dt .
\]
If
\[
Q(x,\cdot)\sim Q(y,\cdot)\qquad\forall x,y\in E,
\]
then \(P_t\) has at most one invariant probability measure. In fact, the weaker condition
\[
Q(x,\cdot)\not\perp Q(y,\cdot)\qquad\forall x,y\in E
\]
already suffices for uniqueness. The classical continuous-time Doob theorem assumes stochastic continuity and \(t_0\)-regularity; \(t_0\)-regular means exactly mutual equivalence of all \(P_{t_0}(x,\cdot)\). Strong Feller plus irreducibility is only a common way to prove this regularity, not a necessary hypothesis. ([page.math.tu-berlin.de](https://page.math.tu-berlin.de/~scheutzow/RLM-2015-026-001-07.pdf))

For the ABLM Dirichlet solution as assumed in the problem, stochastic continuity follows from continuous \(E\)-valued paths. Feller continuity in the initial condition is not supplied by the assumptions, but it is not needed for the uniqueness implication above.

---

## 2. Benchmark: linear OU and smooth bounded drifts

Let \(P_t^0\) denote the linear Dirichlet stochastic heat semigroup. For every deterministic \(x\in E\) and every \(t>0\),
\[
P_t^0(x,\cdot)\sim \gamma \sim \nu,
\]
where \(\gamma\) is the Brownian-bridge law and \(\nu\) is the BZ Gibbs measure. Indeed, in the sine basis,
\[
Q_t e_k=\frac{1-e^{-\pi^2k^2t}}{\pi^2k^2}e_k,\qquad
Q_\infty e_k=\frac1{\pi^2k^2}e_k,
\]
so the transition covariance is equivalent to the invariant covariance, and \(S_t x\) lies in the Cameron-Martin space for every \(x\in L^2\).

For a **bounded measurable drift** \(b\), the smooth-gradient proof works: \(B(u)(\theta)=b(u(\theta))\in L^2(0,1)\) and
\[
\int_0^T\|B(u_s)\|_{L^2}^2\,ds\le T\|b\|_\infty^2.
\]
Girsanov gives equivalence of the nonlinear path law and the OU path law, hence
\[
P_t^b(x,\cdot)\sim P_t^0(x,\cdot)\sim\nu.
\]

For the approximants \(b_n=G_{1/n}\), this is true for each fixed \(n\), but the constants blow up:
\[
\|G_{1/n}\|_\infty=\sqrt{\frac n{2\pi}}.
\]
Thus Girsanov gives no uniform integrability of the fixed-time densities. ABLM’s method deliberately avoids Girsanov/Zvonkin transforms; they note these methods are not useful for their SPDE setting, and their published paper does not treat Dirichlet boundary conditions without extra work, which is exactly why your Dirichlet extension assumption is needed. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2011.13498))

---

## 3. Attempts to extend the smooth proof to the \(\delta_0\) drift

### A. BZ strict association / Dirichlet form route

BZ prove a Hilbert-Schmidt \(L^2(\nu)\)-kernel and resolvent absolute continuity, but the actual Markov kernel is constructed only outside a capacity-zero exceptional set \(N\). In the proof of their Proposition 2.5, the kernel is obtained on \(N^c\), and on \(N\) the resolvent is set to zero for the operator-level statement. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/1105.2779))

So this gives:
\[
P_t(x,\cdot)\sim\nu\quad\text{or at least }P_t(x,\cdot)\ll\nu
\]
only quasi-everywhere, not for every deterministic \(x\). The missing fixed-time statement is still:
\[
P_t(x,N)=0\qquad\forall x\in E,\ t>0.
\]
This is a **fixed-time exceptional-start obstruction**, not merely path-law singularity.

### B. Finite-dimensional Galerkin / BV-potential forms

In finite dimension, BZ reduce the projected singular equation to skew Brownian coordinates plus a bounded drift via Girsanov, and the skew Brownian semigroup has absolute continuity for all initial conditions. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/1105.2779))

However, finite-dimensional fixed-time equivalence does **not** imply infinite-dimensional equivalence. All finite-dimensional projections of two Gaussian measures can be equivalent while the infinite-dimensional measures are singular. Thus one needs a uniform projective overlap, Hellinger, entropy, or \(L^p\) estimate. This is a genuine **fixed-time projective-limit obstruction**.

### C. Scale/skew transform

For one-dimensional skew Brownian motion, a scale transform removes the local-time drift. For the SPDE, a pointwise transform \(v=s(u)\) formally creates terms like
\[
s''(u)(\partial_\theta u)^2
\]
and infinite Itô/renormalization corrections. ABLM explicitly point out that Zvonkin-type transforms are difficult in SPDEs because the Itô formula contains additional renormalized nonlinear terms. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2011.13498))

So the scale route does not currently yield fixed-time equivalence.

### D. Malliavin fixed-time route

For smooth \(b_n=G_{1/n}\), the Malliavin derivative solves a linearized equation with potential
\[
b_n'(r)=-nrG_{1/n}(r).
\]
But
\[
\sup |b_n'|\asymp n,\qquad
\mathbb E|b_n'(X)|\asymp \sqrt n,\qquad
\mathbb E|b_n'(X)|^2\asymp n^{3/2}
\]
for nondegenerate Gaussian \(X\). Standard Malliavin/BEL estimates therefore blow up. A successful proof would need a signed local-time cancellation estimate, not a size estimate.

### E. Approximation alone

The assumed convergence
\[
P_t^n(x,\cdot)\Rightarrow P_t(x,\cdot)
\]
does not preserve absolute continuity. Weak limits of absolutely continuous measures may be singular. Thus the smooth Girsanov proof leaves exactly a uniform-integrability gap at fixed time.

---

## 4. Conditional lemmas weaker than full \(L^p\) smoothing

### Lemma 1: uniform integrability of approximating densities is enough

For fixed \(t>0\), suppose
\[
P_t^n(x,\cdot)=r_{n,t,x}\,\nu
\]
and \(\{r_{n,t,x}\}_n\) is uniformly integrable in \(L^1(\nu)\) for every deterministic \(x\). If \(P_t^n(x,\cdot)\Rightarrow P_t(x,\cdot)\), then
\[
P_t(x,\cdot)\ll\nu.
\]

Entropy is sufficient:
\[
\sup_n\int r_{n,t,x}\log r_{n,t,x}\,d\nu<\infty.
\]
This is strictly weaker than the \(L^p\) criterion in t6. If it holds for every \(x\), then any invariant \(\mu\) satisfies \(\mu\ll\nu\), and the known BZ ergodicity/irreducibility of the symmetric form gives \(\mu=\nu\).

### Lemma 2: projective overlap is enough for Doob uniqueness

Let \(\pi_m:E\to\mathbb R^m\) be Fourier projections generating the Borel sigma-field. Fix \(t_0>0\). If for every \(x,y\in E\) there is \(c(x,y)>0\) such that
\[
\inf_m \int \min\{p_{m,x},p_{m,y}\}\,d\lambda_m\ge c(x,y),
\]
where \(p_{m,x},p_{m,y}\) are densities of
\[
\pi_m\#P_{t_0}(x,\cdot),\qquad \pi_m\#P_{t_0}(y,\cdot)
\]
with respect to any common reference \(\lambda_m\), then \(P_{t_0}(x,\cdot)\not\perp P_{t_0}(y,\cdot)\) for all \(x,y\). Hence uniqueness follows by the non-singular Doob criterion.

Equivalently, it suffices to prove a uniform Hellinger lower bound
\[
\inf_m \int \sqrt{p_{m,x}p_{m,y}}\,d\lambda_m>0.
\]
This is weaker than proving \(P_t(x,\cdot)\sim\nu\).

For approximants, it is enough to prove the same bound uniformly in \(n,m\):
\[
\sup_{n,m}\left\|
\pi_m\#P_{t_0}^n(x,\cdot)-\pi_m\#P_{t_0}^n(y,\cdot)
\right\|_{\mathrm{TV}}
\le 1-c(x,y).
\]

### Lemma 3: exceptional-set leakage suffices

Let \(N\) be the BZ exceptional set and let \(Q=\bar R_\lambda\). Suppose \(Q(x,\cdot)\ll\nu\) for \(x\notin N\), and the substochastic kernel
\[
Q_N(x,A):=Q(x,A\cap N),\qquad x\in N,
\]
has no invariant probability on \(N\). For instance, it is enough that
\[
\sup_{x\in N} Q(x,N)<1.
\]
Then every invariant probability \(\mu\) satisfies \(\mu(N)=0\), hence \(\mu\ll\nu\), and therefore \(\mu=\nu\). This is weaker than proving \(P_t(x,N)=0\) for every \(x,t\).

---

## 5. Sharp remaining estimate

For the Doob route, the sharp target is not full equivalence but merely pairwise non-singularity:
\[
\boxed{
\forall x,y\in E\quad \exists t>0:\quad
P_t(x,\cdot)\not\perp P_t(y,\cdot).
}
\]

A concrete finite-dimensional sufficient estimate is:
\[
\boxed{
\forall x,y\in E\quad \exists t>0,\ c(x,y)>0:\quad
\sup_{n,m}
\left\|\pi_m\#P_t^n(x,\cdot)-\pi_m\#P_t^n(y,\cdot)\right\|_{\mathrm{TV}}
\le 1-c(x,y).
}
\]

A reference-measure alternative is:
\[
\boxed{
\forall x\in E,\ t>0:\quad
\{dP_t^n(x,\cdot)/d\nu\}_n
\text{ is uniformly integrable in }L^1(\nu).
}
\]

Either estimate would complete uniqueness. The present assumptions prove the smooth fixed-\(n\) equivalence, the BZ quasi-everywhere equivalence, and stationary equivalence, but not one of these uniform fixed-time estimates for every deterministic initial state.

## Net outcome

The projected drifts **do have useful uniform-in-\(n\) estimates for every fixed finite projection**, and these estimates are strong enough to give finite-dimensional absolute continuity by Girsanov. However, the constants scale like

\[
\mathbb E\int_0^T |B_{1:m}^n(t)|^2\,dt \asymp \sqrt m,
\]

so the Girsanov route does **not** give the dimension-free \(L^p\) estimate needed in t6. The exact obstruction is that the spatial local-time measure \(\delta_0(u_t(x))\,dx\) has Fourier energy

\[
\mathbb E |B_i(t)|^2 \asymp i^{-1/2},
\qquad 
\sum_{i\le m}\mathbb E |B_i(t)|^2\asymp \sqrt m,
\]

so it is only \(H^{-s}\)-valued for \(s>1/4\), not \(H\)-valued.

Below, I state the estimates with one explicit ABLM-type local nondeterminism input. The stationary lower scaling is unconditional modulo the standard bounded Gibbs density formula for the smooth approximants.

---

# 1. Notation

Let

\[
e_i(x)=\sqrt2\sin(i\pi x),\qquad \lambda_i=(i\pi)^2,
\]

and

\[
b_n(r)=G_{1/n}(r)=\sqrt{\frac n{2\pi}}e^{-nr^2/2}.
\]

For smooth \(h\),

\[
B_h^n(t)=\int_0^1 h(x)b_n(u_t^n(x))\,dx.
\]

For the first \(m\) coordinates,

\[
B_{1:m}^n(t)=\bigl(B_{e_1}^n(t),\dots,B_{e_m}^n(t)\bigr),
\]

and

\[
|B_{1:m}^n(t)|^2
=
\iint_{[0,1]^2} K_m(x,y)b_n(u_t^n(x))b_n(u_t^n(y))\,dx\,dy,
\]

where

\[
K_m(x,y)=\sum_{i=1}^m e_i(x)e_i(y)
\]

is the Dirichlet projection kernel.

---

# 2. Local nondeterminism input

The needed stochastic sewing/local nondeterminism estimate is the following.

For \(z=(t,x)\), set

\[
\rho(z,z')=|x-x'|+|t-t'|^{1/2},
\qquad 
\rho_\partial(z)=x\wedge(1-x)\wedge t^{1/2}.
\]

Assume that for every \(q,T\) and compact \(K\subset C_0([0,1])\), there is \(C_{q,T,K}\) such that for all \(n\), all \(u_0\in K\), and all \(z_1,\dots,z_q\in(0,T]\times(0,1)\), the joint density of \((u^n(z_1),\dots,u^n(z_q))\) satisfies

\[
p_{z_1,\dots,z_q}^{n,u_0}(a_1,\dots,a_q)
\le
C_{q,T,K}
\prod_{j=1}^q r_j^{-1/2},
\]

where, after any fixed ordering,

\[
r_1=\rho_\partial(z_1),\qquad
r_j=\rho_\partial(z_j)\wedge\min_{i<j}\rho(z_j,z_i).
\]

Then, since \(b_n\) is a probability density,

\[
\mathbb E_{u_0}\prod_{j=1}^q b_n(u^n(z_j))
\le
C_{q,T,K}
\prod_{j=1}^q r_j^{-1/2}.
\tag{LND}
\]

This is the only analytic input used below.

---

# 3. Uniform scalar projected-drift moments

Define

\[
A_h^n(T)=\int_0^T |B_h^n(t)|^2\,dt.
\]

For smooth \(h\), set

\[
\mathcal K_T(h)
=
\int_0^T\iint_{[0,1]^2}
|h(x)h(y)|
\Bigl(
t^{-1/2}
+|x-y|^{-1/2}
+x^{-1/2}
+(1-x)^{-1/2}
+y^{-1/2}
+(1-y)^{-1/2}
\Bigr)
\,dx\,dy\,dt.
\]

This is finite for every bounded \(h\), in particular for smooth \(h\).

Using (LND) and the standard Berman local-time moment argument gives, for every integer \(k\ge1\),

\[
\boxed{
\sup_{n\ge1}\sup_{u_0\in K}
\mathbb E_{u_0}\bigl[A_h^n(T)^k\bigr]
\le
k!\,\bigl(C_{T,K}\mathcal K_T(h)\bigr)^k.
}
\tag{3.1}
\]

Consequently,

\[
\boxed{
\sup_{n,u_0\in K}
\mathbb E_{u_0}
\exp\{\theta A_h^n(T)\}
\le
\frac1{1-\theta C_{T,K}\mathcal K_T(h)}
}
\tag{3.2}
\]

for every

\[
0\le \theta < \frac1{C_{T,K}\mathcal K_T(h)}.
\]

Thus each fixed smooth projected drift has uniform-in-\(n\) polynomial moments of all orders and a small exponential moment.

Moreover, \(B_h^n\) converges in \(L^2(\Omega\times[0,T])\) to the weighted spatial local-time functional

\[
B_h(t)=\int_0^1 h(x)\,\delta_0(u_t(x))\,dx,
\]

understood as the \(L^2\)-limit of the mollified occupations.

---

# 4. First \(m\) modes: sharp upper scaling

Let

\[
A_m^n(T)=\int_0^T |B_{1:m}^n(t)|^2\,dt.
\]

The same argument gives

\[
\boxed{
\sup_{n,u_0\in K}
\mathbb E_{u_0}\bigl[A_m^n(T)^k\bigr]
\le
k!\,\bigl(C_{T,K}(1+\sqrt m)\bigr)^k.
}
\tag{4.1}
\]

Hence

\[
\boxed{
\sup_{n,u_0\in K}
\mathbb E_{u_0}
\exp\{\theta A_m^n(T)\}
\le
\frac1{1-\theta C_{T,K}(1+\sqrt m)}
}
\tag{4.2}
\]

for

\[
0\le\theta<\frac1{C_{T,K}(1+\sqrt m)}.
\]

The \(\sqrt m\) comes from the Dirichlet kernel estimate

\[
\iint_{[0,1]^2}
|K_m(x,y)|
\Bigl(1+|x-y|^{-1/2}+x^{-1/2}+(1-x)^{-1/2}\Bigr)
\,dx\,dy
\le C\sqrt m.
\tag{4.3}
\]

The proof uses the standard bound

\[
|K_m(x,y)|
\lesssim
m\wedge |x-y|^{-1}
+
m\wedge (x+y)^{-1}
+
m\wedge (2-x-y)^{-1}.
\]

The singular diagonal term gives exactly \(\sqrt m\):

\[
\int_0^1 (m\wedge r^{-1})r^{-1/2}\,dr
\asymp \sqrt m.
\]

---

# 5. Finite-dimensional Girsanov consequence

Let \(Q_{m,t}^{u_0}\) denote the law at time \(t\) of the first \(m\) coordinates of the linear Ornstein–Uhlenbeck heat equation started from \(u_0\). The projected process satisfies

\[
dY_i^n(t)
=
-\frac{\lambda_i}{2}Y_i^n(t)\,dt
+
B_{e_i}^n(t)\,dt
+
d\beta_i(t).
\]

By finite-dimensional Girsanov, localization, and the energy estimate,

\[
H\!\left(
\mathcal L(Y_{1:m}^n(t))\,\middle|\, Q_{m,t}^{u_0}
\right)
\le
\frac12
\mathbb E_{u_0}\int_0^t |B_{1:m}^n(s)|^2\,ds
\le
C_{t,K}(1+\sqrt m).
\tag{5.1}
\]

Using the exponential estimate (4.2) and the standard reverse Hölder form of Girsanov, there are universal constants \(c,C>0\) such that

\[
p_m:=1+\frac{c}{1+\sqrt m}
\]

satisfies

\[
\boxed{
\sup_{n,u_0\in K}
\left\|
\frac{d\mathcal L(Y_{1:m}^n(t))}
     {dQ_{m,t}^{u_0}}
\right\|_{L^{p_m}(Q_{m,t}^{u_0})}
\le C_{t,K}.
}
\tag{5.2}
\]

The OU law \(Q_{m,t}^{u_0}\) has a density relative to the stationary Gaussian marginal \(\gamma_m\), and for every fixed \(r>1\),

\[
\sup_{m\ge1}\sup_{u_0\in K}
\left\|
\frac{dQ_{m,t}^{u_0}}{d\gamma_m}
\right\|_{L^r(\gamma_m)}
<\infty.
\tag{5.3}
\]

The smooth Gibbs measures \(\nu_n\) satisfy

\[
d\nu_n(u)=Z_n^{-1}\exp\left(2\int_0^1 F_n(u(x))\,dx\right)d\gamma(u),
\qquad F_n'=b_n,
\]

and \(F_n\) is uniformly bounded. Hence every finite-dimensional Gibbs marginal \(\nu_{n,m}\) is uniformly equivalent to \(\gamma_m\).

Combining (5.2), (5.3), and \(\nu_{n,m}\sim\gamma_m\), we obtain:

\[
\boxed{
\pi_m\#P_t^n(u_0,\cdot)\ll \nu_{n,m}
}
\]

and, for

\[
\bar p_m=1+\frac{c'}{1+\sqrt m},
\]

\[
\boxed{
\sup_{n,u_0\in K}
\left\|
\frac{d\,\pi_m\#P_t^n(u_0,\cdot)}
     {d\nu_{n,m}}
\right\|_{L^{\bar p_m}(\nu_{n,m})}
\le C_{t,K}.
}
\tag{5.4}
\]

Thus fixed-dimensional projected absolute continuity is available uniformly in \(n\). But the exponent satisfies

\[
\bar p_m-1\asymp m^{-1/2},
\]

so this does **not** imply the t6 projective \(L^p\) criterion, which needs one \(p>1\) independent of \(m\).

---

# 6. Sharp scaling counterestimate

The \(\sqrt m\) loss is not an artifact.

Let \(\gamma\) be the Brownian bridge law. For \(B\sim\gamma\),

\[
\mathbb E_\gamma b_n(B_x)b_n(B_y)
=
\frac1{2\pi\sqrt{\det(\Sigma_{x,y}+n^{-1}I_2)}},
\]

where \(\Sigma_{x,y}\) is the covariance matrix of \((B_x,B_y)\). As \(n\to\infty\),

\[
\mathbb E_\gamma b_n(B_x)b_n(B_y)
\to
p_0(x,y)
=
\frac1{2\pi\sqrt{(x\wedge y)(1-x\vee y)|x-y|}}.
\]

Near the diagonal,

\[
p_0(x,y)
\sim
\frac1{2\pi\sqrt{x(1-x)}}|x-y|^{-1/2}.
\]

Therefore

\[
a_i
:=
\iint e_i(x)e_i(y)p_0(x,y)\,dx\,dy
\asymp i^{-1/2}.
\tag{6.1}
\]

Equivalently,

\[
\mathbb E_\gamma
\left|
\int_0^1 e_i(x)\delta_0(B_x)\,dx
\right|^2
\asymp i^{-1/2}.
\]

Thus

\[
\sum_{i=1}^m a_i
\asymp
\sqrt m.
\tag{6.2}
\]

Since the smooth Gibbs density \(d\nu_n/d\gamma\) is bounded above and below uniformly in \(n\),

\[
\boxed{
\liminf_{n\to\infty}
\mathbb E_{\nu_n}
|B_{1:m}^n(0)|^2
\ge
c\sqrt m,
}
\tag{6.3}
\]

and similarly

\[
\boxed{
\sup_{n\ge1}
\mathbb E_{\nu_n}
|B_{1:m}^n(0)|^2
\le
C\sqrt m.
}
\tag{6.4}
\]

By stationarity,

\[
\boxed{
\liminf_{n\to\infty}
\mathbb E_{\nu_n}
\int_0^T |B_{1:m}^n(t)|^2\,dt
\ge
cT\sqrt m.
}
\tag{6.5}
\]

So no dimension-free estimate of the form

\[
\sup_{m,n}
\mathbb E\int_0^T |B_{1:m}^n(t)|^2\,dt<\infty
\]

can hold.

Likewise, the full \(H\)-energy diverges with \(n\):

\[
\mathbb E_\gamma \|b_n(B)\|_{L^2(0,1)}^2
=
\int_0^1
\mathbb E_\gamma b_n(B_x)^2\,dx
\asymp
\sqrt n.
\tag{6.6}
\]

This is the precise quantitative reason full \(H\)-valued Girsanov fails.

---

# 7. Sobolev interpretation

Because

\[
\mathbb E |B_i|^2\asymp i^{-1/2},
\]

we have

\[
\sum_{i\ge1}\lambda_i^{-s}\mathbb E |B_i|^2
\asymp
\sum_{i\ge1} i^{-2s-1/2}.
\]

This converges iff

\[
2s+\frac12>1,
\qquad\text{i.e.}\qquad
s>\frac14.
\]

Thus the limiting projected drift is naturally \(H^{-s}\)-valued for every \(s>1/4\), but not \(H\)-valued. This is exactly consistent with the \(\sqrt m\) growth of the first \(m\) Fourier modes.

---

# 8. Conclusion

The finite-dimensional projected drift estimates are good enough to prove:

\[
\pi_m\#P_t^n(u_0,\cdot)\ll \nu_{n,m}
\]

uniformly in \(n\) for each fixed \(m\), with an \(L^{1+c/\sqrt m}\) density bound.

But the sharp energy scaling

\[
\mathbb E\int_0^T |B_{1:m}^n(t)|^2\,dt\asymp \sqrt m
\]

prevents this Girsanov method from giving a projective \(L^p\) bound with one \(p>1\) independent of \(m\). Therefore this route does not close the t6 criterion. Any proof of the full deterministic-start smoothing must exploit cancellations beyond quadratic projected drift energy.
